What's Enhanced from ECC:

  • OData V2 embedded by default 
  • OData V4 support via RAP framework (new capability)
  • Pre-built released APIs in SAP Business Accelerator Hub
  • HANA-optimized query performance

Why OData Matters

  • REST over HTTP
  • JSON-first
  • Queryable data access
  • Self-describing APIs

Key Characteristics:

  • RESTful: Standard HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE)
  • JSON/XML: Modern data formats
  • Query-capable: $filter, $select, $expand, $orderby, $top, $skip
  • Self-documenting: $metadata endpoint
  • Stateless: No session management needed

SAP Gateway Architecture:

 

Standard S/4HANA APIs (Examples):

API Service

Business Purpose

Key Entity Sets

API_PURCHASEORDER_PROCESS_SRV

Purchase Order lifecycle management

A_PurchaseOrder, A_PurchaseOrderItem

API_PRODUCT_SRV

Material / Product master data

A_Product, A_ProductDescription

API_SALES_ORDER_SRV

Sales order processing

A_SalesOrder, A_SalesOrderItem

API_BUSINESS_PARTNER

Unified business partner model

A_BusinessPartner, A_BPContactAddress

API_MATERIAL_STOCK_SRV

Inventory and stock levels

A_MaterialStock

Technical Details:

  • Service Registration: /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE
  • Testing: /IWFND/GW_CLIENT
  • Development: SEGW (Gateway Service Builder)
  • Monitoring: /IWFND/ERROR_LOG

OData Query Capabilities

Basic Read

GET /sap/opu/odata/sap/API_PURCHASEORDER_PROCESS_SRV/A_PurchaseOrder

Filtering

GET .../A_PurchaseOrder

?$filter=DocumentDate ge datetime'2025-01-01'

 and PurchaseOrderType eq 'NB'

Selecting Specific Fields

GET .../A_PurchaseOrder

?$select=PurchaseOrder,Supplier,DocumentDate,TotalAmount

Expanding Navigation Properties

GET .../A_PurchaseOrder('4500000001')

?$expand=to_PurchaseOrderItem

Pagination

GET .../A_PurchaseOrder?$top=100&$skip=0

Response:

"__next": "...&$skiptoken=100"

Metadata Discovery

GET /sap/opu/odata/sap/API_PURCHASEORDER_PROCESS_SRV/$metadata

Returns:

  • Entity sets
  • Properties
  • Associations

When to Use OData

Use When

  • Modern web or mobile applications
  • Real-time queries required
  • REST/JSON integration
  • Flexible query support needed

EXAMPLE SCENARIO: Mobile Sales App Querying Products

Business Context:

Sales representatives use mobile app to check product availability, pricing, and specifications while visiting customers. Need instant access to product catalog with real-time stock levels.

Technical Setup:

  • Source: React Native mobile app
  • Target: S/4HANA On-Premise
  • API: API_PRODUCT_SRV, API_MATERIAL_STOCK_SRV
  • Frequency: Real-time as sales rep searches
  • Volume: 500-1,000 queries per day

Process Flow:

  1. Sales rep searches "Bearing" in mobile app
  2. App sends HTTP GET to CPI endpoint
  3. CPI forwards to S/4HANA Gateway
  4. Gateway routes to Data Provider Class
  5. DPC queries HANA database (ultra-fast)
  6. Returns matching products as JSON
  7. For each product, fetch stock level
  8. Combine product + stock data
  9. Response returns to mobile app
  10. Rep sees list: 15 products with prices and stock

3.3 CDS Views as Integration Endpoints

Core Data Services (CDS) views are HANA-optimized semantic data models defined in S/4HANA. They represent business entities and logic at the database level and can be exposed directly for integration.

CDS views can be:

  • Exposed as OData services via SAP Gateway
  • Consumed internally or externally via RFC-enabled function modules

Key Benefits

  • High performance: Logic is pushed down to the HANA database
  • Pre-aggregated data: Reduces processing in the application layer
  • Embedded business logic: Calculations, annotations, and semantics included
  • Rich relationships: Associations allow navigation across related entities

Process Flow

CDS View → Gateway Service → OData → Integration Suite

3.4 SAP Business Events & Real-Time Integration

S/4HANA introduces native event-driven integration capabilities, allowing systems to react immediately to business changes instead of relying on batch jobs or polling.

Key enhancements include:

  • Standardized business event triggers
  • Improved change detection
  • Workflow-driven event publishing
  • Native integration with SAP Event Mesh

Note: Event Mesh provides additional capabilities like publish/subscribe, guaranteed delivery queues, and multiple independent subscribers.

Event-Driven Integration Pattern

S/4HANA Business Event → Event Mesh Topic → Multiple Subscribers (CPI iFlows) → External Systems

Typical Use Cases

  • Purchase order approved → Notify procurement portal
  • Stock level falls below threshold → Trigger replenishment
  • Sales order created → Update CRM system
  • Invoice posted → Notify payment or reporting systems

EXAMPLE SCENARIO:

Real-Time Stock Alerts to E-Commerce

E-commerce website needs instant notifications when product stock falls below threshold to display "Low Stock" warnings and trigger replenishment.

Technical Setup:

  • Source: S/4HANA On-Premises
  • Targets: E-commerce website, Replenishment system, Analytics dashboard
  • Event: Material.StockChanged
  • Frequency: Real-time (as goods movements occur)
  • Volume: 2,000-3,000 events per day

Step-by-Step Flow

  1. Goods Issue Posted
    • A goods issue is posted in S/4HANA using transaction MIGO.
  2. Stock Update in HANA
    • The stock quantity is immediately updated in the HANA database.
  3. Business Event Triggered
    • The system triggers the business event Material.StockChanged.
  4. Event Published
    • The event is published to SAP Event Mesh.
    • Topic: inventory/changes
  5. Parallel Event Consumption
    • Multiple systems subscribe to the same event and process it independently.

Subscriber A – E-Commerce System

  • Receives the event through a CPI iFlow
  • Updates the product page inventory
  • Displays a “Low Stock” indicator (e.g., “Only 75 left”)

Subscriber B – Replenishment System

  • Evaluates stock against reorder threshold (100 units)
  • If below threshold:
    • Creates a Purchase Requisition
    • Notifies the purchasing team

Subscriber C – Analytics Dashboard

  • Updates real-time inventory charts
  • Stores event data in the data warehouse for reporting
  1. Independent Processing
    • Each subscriber processes the event independently and in parallel.
  2. Acknowledgement
    • Each system sends an acknowledgement back to Event Mesh.

 

Events are published once and delivered to multiple consumers in parallel, without tight coupling.

4. INTEGRATION SCENARIOS & COMPARISON

Now that we understand what each system offers, let's look at how to choose the right protocol and see complete integration scenarios.

4.2  Decision Tree

4.3 Cross-Protocol Integration Scenarios

 

Scenario 1: Manufacturing Company - Complete Landscape

Integration Breakdown

  1. Web Portal → SAP ECC (Purchase Orders)
    • Protocol: REST → RFC/BAPI
    • Purpose: Real-time purchase order creation
    • Volume: ~100 purchase orders per day
  2. SAP ECC → Data Warehouse (Material Master)
    • Protocol: IDoc → JSON
    • Purpose: Nightly master data synchronization
    • Volume: ~10,000 materials per night
  3. Mobile App → SAP S/4HANA (Product Catalog)
    • Protocol: REST → OData
    • Purpose: Real-time product search and availability
    • Volume: ~1,000 queries per day
  4. SAP S/4HANA → E-Commerce Platform (Stock Alerts)
    • Protocol: Business Events via Event Mesh
    • Purpose: Real-time inventory notifications
    • Volume: ~3,000 events per day

5. System Preparation Checklists

Before starting integration development, systems must be technically and operationally ready. The following checklists reflect production-grade prerequisites.

5.1 SAP ECC Preparation

Technical Configuration

RFC Destinations (SM59)
 ☐   HTTP/RFC destination created for Integration Suite
 ☐   Connection test successful
 ☐   Timeout parameters adjusted
 ☐   Trusted RFC configured (if applicable)

IDoc Configuration (If IDocs Are Used)
 ☐   Partner profiles maintained (WE20)
   ☐   Inbound parameters
   ☐   Outbound parameters
   ☐   Message types assigned
 ☐   Ports configured (WE21)
  ☐   HTTP port to Integration Suite
  ☐   Port connectivity tested
 ☐   Message control configured (for outbound IDocs)
  ☐   Application (ME, VL, VF, etc.)
  ☐   Output type
  ☐   Medium = 6 (IDoc)
 ☐   Test IDocs generated successfully (WE19)

Web Services (SOAMANAGER)
 ☐   Service definitions created
 ☐   Endpoints configured and reachable
 ☐   WSDL accessible externally
 ☐   Authentication configured

User & Authorization
 ☐   Technical user created (SU01)
 ☐   Required authorizations assigned:
  ☐   S_RFC
  ☐   S_IDOCDEFT
  ☐   Relevant business authorizations
 ☐   Password policy compliant
 ☐   Dialog access restricted for security

Network & Security

Firewall Rules
 ☐   Outbound: ECC → Integration Suite (HTTPS 443)
 ☐   Inbound: Integration Suite → ECC (HTTP/HTTPS)
 ☐   IP whitelisting completed

SSL / TLS Configuration
  ☐   Certificates imported (STRUST)
  ☐   Certificate chain verified
  ☐   Expiry monitoring in place

Cloud Connector (If On-Premise Connectivity Is Required)
  ☐   Installed and connected to BTP subaccount
  ☐   Access control rules defined
  ☐   Resource mapping configured
  ☐   Audit logging enabled

5.2 SAP S/4HANA Preparation

All ECC prerequisites apply, plus the following.

OData / Gateway Configuration

  ☐   Services activated (/IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE)
  ☐   System alias assigned
  ☐   Service registration complete
  ☐   ICF nodes active (SICF)
 ☐   Service testing completed (/IWFND/GW_CLIENT)
  ☐   $metadata accessible
  ☐   Entity sets queryable
  ☐   CRUD operations verified
 ☐   Authorization configured (S_SERVICE, PFCG roles)

Custom OData Services (If Applicable)
 ☐   Data model designed (SEGW)
 ☐   DPC_EXT implemented
 ☐   Runtime objects generated
 ☐   Service registered and tested

Event Configuration (If Event-Driven Integration Is Used)
 ☐   Business events identified
 ☐   Event Mesh connectivity established
 ☐   Topic and queue structure defined
 ☐   Payload format designed
 ☐   End-to-end testing completed.

CDS View Exposure
 ☐   CDS views created and optimized
 ☐   Exposed via OData
 ☐   Authorization validated
 ☐   Performance tested.

5.3 SAP BTP Integration Suite Preparation

Platform Enablement

 ☐   Integration Suite provisioned
 ☐   Cloud Integration enabled
 ☐   API Management enabled (if required)
 ☐   Event Mesh enabled (if required)
 ☐   Integration Advisor enabled (if B2B scenarios)

Connectivity

 ☐   Destinations configured
   ☐   HTTP destinations to ECC/S/4HANA
   ☐   Authentication defined
   ☐   Proxy type set (Internet / On-Premise)
 ☐   Cloud Connector configured
   ☐   Location ID assigned
   ☐   Virtual host mapping
   ☐   Access control lists maintained

Security

 ☐   Credentials stored securely
 ☐   OAuth tokens configured (if applicable)
 ☐   Certificates uploaded and validated
 ☐   Secure parameters used for sensitive values
 ☐   Known hosts configured for SFTP

Design & Operations

 ☐   Integration packages created
 ☐   Naming conventions defined
 ☐   Versioning and transport strategy agreed
 ☐   Message monitoring enabled
 ☐   Alerts configured
 ☐   Log retention policy set
 ☐   Operational dashboards created

6. INTEGRATION ARCHITECTURE OVERVIEW

Understanding how all pieces fit together:

Component Explanations

Cloud Connector

  • Provides a secure tunnel between SAP BTP and on-premise SAP systems
  • Eliminates the need for VPN connectivity
  • Enables resource-level access control
  • Supports audit logging for compliance and security monitoring

CPI Adapters

  • Enable protocol translation (RFC ↔ REST, IDoc ↔ JSON, SOAP ↔ HTTP)
  • Handle authentication and credential management
  • Support connection pooling for performance optimization
  • Provide built-in retry mechanisms for transient failures

API Management

  • Enforces rate limiting and quotas
  • Supports API key and OAuth-based validation
  • Offers analytics and usage monitoring
  • Includes a developer portal for API discovery and onboarding

Event Mesh

  • Supports publish / subscribe messaging
  • Provides queue-based guaranteed delivery
  • Enables topic-based routing
  • Allows multiple subscribers to process events independently

7. Key Takeaways

Understanding System Capabilities

SAP ECC Strengths

  • Mature and stable RFC/BAPI framework
  • Robust IDoc infrastructure for asynchronous processing
  • Battle-tested reliability in enterprise landscapes
  • Rich Enterprise Services Repository

SAP S/4HANA Advantages

  • Native OData / REST APIs
  • Built-in event-driven integration
  • High performance powered by SAP HANA
  • CDS views for optimized data access
  • Includes everything ECC offers — enhanced and modernized

SAP BTP Integration Suite as the Bridge

  • Centralized protocol translation
  • Secure cloud connectivity
  • End-to-end orchestration layer
  • Enterprise-grade monitoring and governance

Protocol Selection Guidance

  • RFC / BAPI: Synchronous transactions with immediate validation
  • IDoc: Bulk data processing with guaranteed delivery
  • OData: REST APIs for modern applications
  • SOAP: Legacy enterprise web services
  • File Interfaces: Batch processing and data migrations
  • Events: Real-time, decoupled integration (S/4HANA)

Preparation Is Critical

  • System configuration must be completed before development
  • Security and authorizations must be production-ready
  • Network connectivity should be fully tested
  • Monitoring and alerting should be established early

8. What’s Next

This blog explained what is possible.
The next blogs series will be focused on how to implement.

 

Acknowledgments & Corrections

This document was refined based on valuable feedback from the SAP Community, particularly regarding:

  • NetWeaver Gateway as a standard, supported component in ECC
  • JSON support in OData V2 for both ECC and S/4HANA
  • BAPI transaction control requirements
  • Event architecture distinctions

I welcome continued feedback to ensure this remains an accurate, community-validated resource.

entry.title: Step-By-Step Guide: Creating Your First RAP Application using OData V4 and Transporting to Quality
entry.content:
Introduction

In this blog, we will walk through the detailed steps to create your first RAP (RESTful ABAP Programming Model) application and correctly move it to the Quality system. This blog also highlights mandatory steps before transport and common mistakes that beginners usually make, based on real project experience.

Target Audience & Prerequisites

  • ABAP developers who are new to RAP
  • Basic knowledge of CDS Views and ABAP Development Tools (ADT)
  • System used: SAP S/4HANA (On-Premise / Private Cloud)
  • Exposure type: OData V4
  • Scenario covered: Basic Read-only RAP application

    Mandatory Objects in a RAP Application:

    • Package
    • Interface CDS View
    • Projection CDS View (Optional we can directly create service definition on Interface view also)
    • Service Definition
    • Service Binding
  1. Create Package: 

    Create a package in ADT:

    • Right-click on project → New → ABAP Package
    • Enter package name and description
    • Do not create it as a local package ($TMP)
    • Capture it in a transport request     
    • Only transportable packages can be moved to Quality and Production systems.

    •  

  2. Interface CDS View: A CDS View is a virtual data model in ABAP that defines how data from database tables is accessed and represented. In RAP, it often serves as the “Interface View.”

    1. Why It Matters:

      • Reusable logic: Centralizes data access for transactional and analytical purposes.
      • Foundation: Serves as the starting point for business object modeling in RAP.

    2. Steps:

      • Right-click your database table (e.g., ZTRAVEL_RAJ_M), select New Data Definition.
      • Provide a name, description, and use the Define View Entity template.
      • Assign to transport, activate, and check for errors.

      •  

  3. Service Definition: The Service Definition specifies which data elements (entities) of a RAP business object are exposed as OData services.

    1. Why It Matters:

      • Exposure Layer: Decides what consumers (e.g., Fiori apps, external APIs) can interact with.
      • It’s the contract between your backend logic and external consumers.
                                                                                                         

    2. Steps:

      • Right-click on the projection view, select New Service Definition.
      • Name, description, assign transport, and activate.
      • This exposes your RAP objects as a service.

      •  

  4. Service Binding: Service Binding links a service definition to an actual communication protocol (such as OData V4) and activation state.
    1. Why It Matters:
      • Enables Consumption: Without binding, your service cannot be called or used.
      • Protocol Choice: Determines how front ends communicate with your service (e.g., OData V4 for Fiori/UI5 apps).

    2. Steps:

      • Right-click on the service definition, select New Service Binding.
      • Choose OData V4 protocol (for most Fiori/RAP apps).
      • Assign transport, describe, finish, and activate.

      •  

      •  

  5. Why You Should NOT Publish from ADT: Publishing directly from ADT is meant only for local testing.

    1. Issues with ADT Publishing

      • Service is registered as a local ($TMP) object
      • Cannot be transported to Quality or Production
      • Not supported for productive landscapes

    2. Correct Way: Publish via /IWFND/V4_ADMIN

      • Transaction /IWFND/V4_ADMIN is the recommended way to publish RAP services.

    3. Steps: 
      • Open /IWFND/V4_ADMIN and click Publish Service Groups

      •  

      • Enter System Alias = LOCAL, Service Binding name click on get service groups

      •  

      • Select service and Publish

      •  

      • From the service group list, right click on your service group and click on transport publishing. It will prompt for customizing TR enter the transport and save. After that Click on transport routing assignment and capture in the customizing TR.

      •  

      •  

      • Service is now published

      •  

  6. Preview: Finally, let’s preview the application to verify that the service is working as expected. 

    •  

Conclusion: 

  • In this blog, we learned how to create a basic RAP application and the correct approach to publish and transport OData V4 services to the Quality system.
  • Using /IWFND/V4_ADMIN ensures transport safety and helps avoid common deployment issues that beginners often face.

Note: 

To customize the user interface of your RAP application, add appropriate UI annotations in the CDS view (interface or projection, depending on your design). Common annotations include @ui.headerInfo, @ui.lineItem, and @ui.selectionField.

After publishing the service, share the service URL (available in the Service Binding) with the Fiori development team so they can create the required Fiori tile.
After transporting to the Quality system, SICF activation is a manual step. Without activating the relevant SICF nodes, the service will not be accessible. Coordinate with your Basis team to complete this step.

entry.title: Connecting SAP Sales Cloud V2 to a Standalone SAP Analytics Cloud - A Practical Alternative
entry.content:
The Customer Challenge

A customer approached me with what seemed like a straightforward request: "We want to analyze our SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 data in SAP Analytics Cloud."

Their situation was common for mid-sized companies adopting SAP's CX portfolio:

  • SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 was live and running well
  • SAP Analytics Cloud was licensed separately (not the embedded version)
  • No SAP BTP subscription yet, it wasn't part of the initial scope
  • No SAP Integration Suite same reason
  • No SAP Datasphere, they wanted to avoid another data platform

The goal was simple: import Accounts, Contacts, and Opportunities into SAC for custom dashboards that combined CX data with data from other sources

What SAP Officially Recommends

Before building anything custom, I researched SAP's official guidance. The recommended architecture for Sales Cloud V2 analytics integration involves:

  1. SAP Datasphere as a central data layer
  2. SAP Integration Suite for event-driven data replication
  3. SAP Advanced Event Mesh (optional but recommended) for resilience

SAP published a detailed two-part blog series on this: Event-Driven Data Integration from SAP Sales and Service Cloud V2 to SAP Datasphere

This architecture is elegant and production-grade. It's also significant additional investment for a customer who just wants to run a few reports.

Cost Implications

The official path requires more than you might expect:

ComponentStatus for this Customer
SAP DatasphereNot licensed (€€€/month)
SAP Integration SuiteNot licensed (€€/month)
SAP BTP subscriptionNot available
Implementation effortSignificant (weeks)


For a customer with ~24,000 accounts who wants three dashboards, this felt like using a sledgehammer to crack a nut.

The Obvious First Attempt: SAC's Built-in Connectors

SAC has connectors for various SAP sources. Surely there's something for Sales Cloud?

Attempt 1: The "SAP Cloud for Customer" Connector

SAC includes a dedicated connector called "SAP Cloud for Customer" that works beautifully with the older C4C (Cloud for Customer) system. It connects to OData services like:

https://<tenant>/sap/c4c/odata/v1/c4codataapi/AccountCollection

The problem: Sales Cloud V2 is not C4C. So the OData connector won’t work:

Attempt 2: The Generic OData Connector

SAC also has a generic "OData Services" connector. Maybe that works?

The problem: Sales Cloud V2 doesn't expose OData endpoints. The APIs on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub are REST, not OData.

GET /sap/c4c/api/v1/account-service/accounts ← REST, not OData
GET /sap/c4c/api/v1/contact-service/contacts ← REST, not OData

Attempt 3: Live Connection

Sales Cloud V2 has embedded SAC capabilities. Could we establish a live connection?

The problem: The embedded analytics are designed for "embedded" use cases. Connecting a standalone SAC tenant to Sales Cloud V2 for custom reporting seems to be... let's say "not a focus area" right now.

I opened a support ticket to clarify the expected behavior and it was put in development after a little back and forth.

The Hidden Challenge: Real-Time vs. Import

At this point, I had to step back and discuss architecture with the customer.

Even if a direct connector existed, there are implications to consider:

 

ApproachProsCons
Live ConnectionAlways current dataQuery performance depends on source; cannot combine with other data sources easily
Import ConnectionFast queries; can blend with other sourcesData has latency; storage costs

The customer wanted to combine Sales Cloud V2 data with SAP S/4HANA data in the same dashboard. This essentially requires import connections for both sources, plus a data model in SAC that joins them.

Real-time was never really an option as they needed data at rest in SAP Analytics Cloud 

The Solution: A Lightweight REST-to-OData Proxy

Since the customer had:

  • No appetite for SAP Datasphere
  • No CPI license
  • A clear, limited scope (three entity types)
  • Tolerance for daily data refresh (not real-time)

I proposed building a thin proxy server that:

  1. Authenticates with Sales Cloud V2
  2. Fetches data via REST APIs
  3. Transforms responses to OData format
  4. Exposes endpoints that SAC can consume natively

Architecture

This sounds straightforward... until you start building it...

Challenge 1: The 999 vs. 1000 Mismatch

This one deserves its own section because it’s so unexpected.

SAP Sales Cloud V2 REST APIs have a maximum page size of 999 records. You can request $top=999, but not more.

SAP Analytics Cloud OData import has a minimum batch size of 1000 records.

Yes, you read that correctly. The source maxes out at 999. The target starts at 1000. I'll admit I stared at the logs for longer than I'd like to admit before I realized what was happening. The first 999 records imported perfectly. Then nothing. It took me a while to connect that SAC was waiting for a 1000-record batch that would never arrive.

The Solution

The proxy implements on-demand parallel fetching:

async function fetchAllRecords(entityType: string): Promise<any[]> {
  // Get first page to determine total count
  const firstPage = await fetchFromSalesCloud(entityType, {
    $top: 999,
    $skip: 0
  });
  
  if (firstPage.value.length < 999) {
    // All records fit in first page
    return firstPage.value;
  }
  
  // Calculate remaining pages and fetch in parallel
  const totalEstimate = firstPage.totalCount || 10000;
  const remainingPages = Math.ceil((totalEstimate - 999) / 999);
  
  const pagePromises = Array.from({ length: remainingPages }, (_, i) => 
    fetchFromSalesCloud(entityType, {
      $top: 999,
      $skip: 999 * (i + 1)
    })
  );
  
  const results = await Promise.all(pagePromises);
  return [firstPage.value, ...results.map(r => r.value)].flat();
}

The proxy fetches pages in parallel (not sequentially), caches the combined result, and serves it to SAC in appropriately sized batches. The cache has a configurable TTL (e.g., 1 hour) and automatically refreshes on the next request after expiry.

Challenge 2: Nested Objects and Flattening

Sales Cloud V2 returns nicely structured JSON with nested objects:

{
  "value": [{
    "accountId": "A001",
    "accountName": "ACME Corp",
    "primaryContact": {
      "contactId": "C001",
      "firstName": "John",
      "lastName": "Doe"
    },
    "addresses": [
      { "type": "BILL_TO", "city": "Berlin" },
      { "type": "SHIP_TO", "city": "Munich" }
    ]
  }]
}

SAC’s OData importer cannot handle this. The SAP Help documentation states clearly: Embedded Complex types are not supported.

The Solution

The proxy must flatten nested structures into separate entity sets:

/odata/Accounts         → Flat account records
/odata/Contacts         → Flat contact records  
/odata/AccountAddresses → One row per address, linked by accountId

Challenge 3: REST APIs Behave Differently Than OData

Coming from the OData world, I expected certain conventions:

  • $filter for filtering
  • $select for field selection
  • $expand for related entities
  • Standardized metadata endpoint

Sales Cloud V2 REST APIs have their own conventions:

  • Different query parameter syntax
  • No $metadata endpoint
  • Pagination via custom headers or response properties

The proxy needs to translate between these worlds, implementing just enough OData semantics to satisfy SAC’s importer.

Challenge 4: The $skip 10,000 Limit

Just when you think you’ve handled pagination, there’s another surprise waiting for datasets with more than 10,000 records.

Sales Cloud V2 returns an error if $skip is 10,000 or greater.

// This works
GET /accounts?$top=999&$skip=9000  ✅

// This fails
GET /accounts?$top=999&$skip=10000  ❌ Error!

So even if you’ve cleverly worked around the 999 page size limit, you hit a wall at record 10,001. Simple offset-based pagination ($skip) just stops working.

The Solution

Instead of offset-based pagination, the proxy uses keyset pagination with $skiptoken:

// src/constants.ts
export const SC_MAX_SKIP = 10000;

// When approaching the skip limit, switch to skiptoken
async function fetchWithKeysetPagination(entityType: string): Promise<any[]> {
  const allRecords: any[] = [];
  let nextLink: string | null = buildInitialUrl(entityType);
  
  while (nextLink) {
    const response = await fetch(nextLink);
    const data = await response.json();
    
    allRecords.push(...data.value);
    
    // Use the skiptoken from the response for next page
    nextLink = data['@odata.nextLink'] || null;
  }
  
  return allRecords;
}

Keyset pagination uses a token (typically based on the last record’s ID or timestamp) rather than a numeric offset. This approach:

  • Has no upper limit on the number of records
  • Is actually more performant than large offsets
  • Is the pattern SAP recommends for large datasets

If you have more than 10,000 records in any entity, you must use $skiptoken-based pagination. Simple $skip won’t cut it.

Implementation Notes

Technology Choice

I built the proxy with:

  • Node.js + Fastify: Lightweight, fast, excellent TypeScript support
  • On-demand caching: No scheduled jobs; cache fills dynamically on first request
  • Docker deployment: Runs on any container platform (I use Coolify on a VPS)

This is a production-ready setup. The proxy has been running reliably for the customer with no issues.

Smart Caching: On-Demand Parallel Fetching

Rather than running scheduled batch jobs, the proxy uses an on-demand approach:

  1. First page served instantly: When SAC requests data, the proxy immediately returns the first page (up to 999 records) from Sales Cloud V2
  2. Parallel fetching in background: While SAC processes the first page, the proxy fetches remaining pages in parallel
  3. Cache populated for subsequent requests: By the time SAC requests page 2, the data is already cached
async function handleODataRequest(req: Request): Promise<Response> {
  // Check cache first
  if (cache.has(entityType) && !cache.isExpired(entityType)) {
    return serveFromCache(entityType, req.query);
  }
  
  // Fetch first page immediately
  const firstPage = await fetchPage(entityType, 0, 999);
  
  // Start parallel fetching of remaining pages (non-blocking)
  fetchRemainingPagesInBackground(entityType, firstPage.totalCount);
  
  // Return first page immediately
  return formatAsOData(firstPage);
}

This approach has several advantages:

  • No unnecessary background jobs: Data is only fetched when actually needed
  • Fast initial response: User doesn’t wait for full dataset
  • Self-healing cache: Expired cache automatically refreshes on next request

Optional: Deploying to SAP BTP Cloud Foundry

If you already have a BTP subscription with available credits, deploying there can make sense. You’re using infrastructure you’re already paying for. The migration is straightforward:

Standalone Setup Cloud Foundry Setup

Environment variables for credentialsDestination Service binding
In-memory cachePostgreSQL Hyperscaler Option (free tier available)
Direct HTTP endpointXSUAA + AppRouter

Why PostgreSQL if you move to BTP?

On a single-container deployment (VPS e.g. with Coolify), in-memory caching works perfectly. But Cloud Foundry typically runs multiple app instances for high availability. If you need persistence across instances, PostgreSQL Hyperscaler Option is the lightweight choice. It has a free tier (since November 2025) and requires no additional management.

The additional effort for Cloud Foundry deployment is roughly:

  • Add mta.yaml with service bindings (~1 hour)
  • Configure Destination Service for Sales Cloud V2 credentials (~30 min)
  • Add XSUAA for authentication (~1 hour)
  • Test and deploy (~2 hours)

Total: Less than one additional day if you decide BTP is the right fit.

Don’t just default to BTP just because it’s SAP. Evaluate what you already have and what makes sense for your situation. A €5/month VPS running Docker might be exactly right for your use case.

OData Response Format

SAC expects OData V2or V4 responses. The minimum viable format:

{
  "d": {
    "results": [
      { "AccountID": "A001", "AccountName": "ACME Corp", ... },
      { "AccountID": "A002", "AccountName": "Globex", ... }
    ]
  }
}

Plus a $metadata endpoint returning EDMX that describes your entity types.

Authentication

Sales Cloud V2 supports:

  • Basic Authentication (simplest for server-to-server)
  • OAuth 2.0 (more secure, recommended for production)

The proxy stores credentials securely and handles token refresh for OAuth.

Results

After implementing the proxy:

  • SAC successfully imports Accounts, Contacts, and Opportunities
  • Data refreshes nightly via scheduled import
  • Customer can blend CX data with S/4HANA data in unified dashboards
  • No additional SAP platform licenses required
  • Total implementation time: ~2 days

When This Approach Makes Sense

The right solution depends on what you have and what you need. Here’s how I think about it:

A lightweight proxy makes sense when:

  • Limited scope (specific entities, not the entire data model)
  • Import/batch refresh is acceptable (not real-time requirements)
  • You don’t have Datasphere or CPI licensed and don’t need them for other use cases
  • You want to validate the integration quickly before investing in bigger infrastructure

Datasphere + CPI makes sense when:

  • You need event-driven, near real-time data replication
  • You’re integrating many entity types with complex relationships
  • You already have Datasphere or CPI licensed then use what you’re paying for
  • You have a broader data strategy where Datasphere is the central hub anyway

The key question: What do you already have, and does this use case justify adding more?

SAP’s platforms are excellent, no question! But sometimes a focused solution that solves exactly your problem is better than pulling in the big guns for a straightforward requirement.

Lessons Learned

1. Don’t Assume SAP Products Integrate Seamlessly

Sales Cloud V2 and SAC are both modern SAP cloud products. The integration gap exists because V2’s move away from OData wasn’t accompanied by a new native SAC connector (yet).

2. Check API Patterns Before Committing to Architecture

The shift from OData to REST in Sales Cloud V2 is documented, but easy to miss if you’re coming from C4C experience. Always verify on api.sap.com.

3. Test with Realistic Data Volumes

The 999/1000 mismatch only surfaces with more than 999 records. Demo systems with 50 accounts work fine. Production breaks.

4. Use What You Have, Validate What You Need

The official architecture is powerful. But if you don’t already have Datasphere and CPI licensed, ask yourself: does this specific use case justify adding them? Sometimes a focused solution that solves exactly your problem is the right call. You can always scale up later if requirements grow.

What’s Next?

I’ve published a reference implementation on GitHub that demonstrates the core patterns:

  • REST-to-OData transformation
  • Parallel fetching with caching
  • The 999→1000 batch bridging logic
  • Keyset pagination with $skiptoken for large datasets (>10k records)

👉 GitHub: SAP Sales Cloud V2 OData Proxy

A note on scope: The repository shows how the solution works, but doesn’t include everything from my production implementation. Some aspects are tied to specific client configurations that I can’t share. If you’re facing a similar challenge and want help getting to production faster, feel free to reach out.

If you’ve built something similar or found a better approach I’d love to hear about it in the comments.

entry.title: Building an Email-Based SAP Sales Order Automation with n8n & AI Function Calling
entry.content:
Building an Email-Based SAP Sales Order Assistant with AI Function Calling

The Problem That Started This

A few weeks ago, I was watching a colleague struggle with a simple request: "Can you check if order 10850 shipped?" They had to VPN in, open SAP GUI, navigate to VA03, enter the order number, and finally get the answer. For one question. That took 3 minutes.

I thought - what if they could just send an email and get an answer back?

Why Not Just Use Rules or Keywords?

My first instinct was simple pattern matching. Look for "create order" then run the create flow. Look for "check status" then run the query flow. Easy, right?

Then I looked at actual emails people send:

  • "Hey, can you set up an order for 17100003? They need 5 of TG11 and 10 of TG12"
  • "pls make SO for cust 17100003 - TG11 x5, TG12 x10"
  • "Customer 17100003 wants to place an order. Materials: TG11 (5 units), TG12 (10 units)"

Same request, completely different wording. Building regex patterns for every variation? That's a maintenance nightmare waiting to happen. And what about typos, abbreviations, or when someone writes in a mix of languages?

This is exactly where AI shines - understanding intent regardless of how it's expressed.

Why Function Calling Instead of Free-Form AI?

I didn't want the AI making up SAP field names or guessing at API structures. That's a recipe for errors. Function calling gives you the best of both worlds:

  • The AI handles the messy, unpredictable part (understanding what the user wants)
  • The code handles the precise, structured part (calling SAP correctly)

You define exactly what functions exist and what parameters they accept. The AI picks the right function and extracts the right values. No hallucinated field names, no invented API calls.

The Architecture

Four components working together:

  1. Microsoft Outlook - Triggers when an email arrives
  2. LLM with Function Calling - Interprets the email, picks the operation
  3. n8n - Orchestrates everything, handles data transformation
  4. SAP S/4HANA OData APIs - Does the actual work

 

Setting Up n8n with Docker (The Network Gotchas)

I run n8n locally in Docker, and connecting it to an on-premise SAP system over VPN was... an adventure. Here's what I learned.

The Basic Setup

docker run -it --rm \
  --name n8n \
  -p 5678:5678 \
  --add-host=host.docker.internal:host-gateway \
  -v n8n_data:/home/node/.n8n \
  docker.n8n.io/n8nio/n8n

That --add-host=host.docker.internal:host-gateway flag is crucial. It lets containers reference your host machine's network. Without it, your container is isolated and can't see anything your host can see - including VPN connections.

The VPN Problem

Here's the thing: when you connect to a VPN on your Mac, the VPN routes are available to your host machine, but Docker containers live in their own network namespace. They can't directly reach 172.16.0.11:44300 even though your browser can.

You might think "just use --network host" - but on macOS, that doesn't work the way you'd expect. Docker Desktop on Mac runs containers in a Linux VM, so --network host gives you the VM's network, not your Mac's network.

The socat Bridge Solution

The workaround is to run a TCP forwarder on your host that bridges the connection:

socat TCP-LISTEN:15001,fork,reuseaddr TCP:172.16.0.11:44300

This listens on port 15001 on your Mac and forwards traffic to the SAP server at 172.16.0.11:44300. Now from inside Docker, you can reach SAP at:

https://host.docker.internal:15001/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_SALES_ORDER_SRV/A_SalesOrder

The flow is: Docker container → host.docker.internal:15001 → socat on your Mac → VPN → SAP server

Keep the socat command running in a terminal while you're working. Kill it when you're done.

Quick Tip: If you're getting "connection refused" errors, check that:

  1. Your VPN is connected
  2. socat is running
  3. You're using host.docker.internal, not localhost or the direct IP

The Functions I Defined

Three operations cover most daily requests:

get_sales_orders

"Show me recent orders," "What's the status of order X?"

Parameters: $top, $filter, $orderby

create_sales_order

"Create an order for customer X with these items"

Parameters: SoldToParty, items[] (Material, RequestedQuantity), plus optional fields

update_sales_order

"Change the PO reference on order 10904"

Parameters: SalesOrder (required), plus any fields to update

When an email comes in, the AI returns structured JSON:

{
  "function": "create_sales_order",
  "arguments": {
    "SoldToParty": "17100003",
    "items": [
      {"Material": "TG11", "RequestedQuantity": "5"},
      {"Material": "TG12", "RequestedQuantity": "10"}
    ]
  }
}

Clean, predictable, easy to work with.

The CSRF Token Headache

I have to mention this because it cost me hours. SAP OData APIs require CSRF tokens for write operations. Sounds simple - fetch a token, send it with your POST. But here's what the documentation doesn't make obvious:

The token is tied to your session cookies. You need to:

  1. GET request with x-csrf-token: fetch header
  2. Capture the token AND the SAP_SESSIONID cookie from the response
  3. Send BOTH with your write request

I kept getting "CSRF token validation failed" with a perfectly valid token. Turns out I was missing the session cookie. The token alone is useless without it.

Another gotcha in n8n

The cookie header comes back with extra metadata like path=/; secure; HttpOnly. If you pass that whole string in your Cookie header, you'll get "Invalid character in header content." You need to extract just the cookie value:

// In an Edit Fields node, clean the cookie
$json.headers['set-cookie'][0].split(';')[0] + '; ' + 
$json.headers['set-cookie'][1].split(';')[0]

This gives you a clean sap-usercontext=sap-client=100; SAP_SESSIONID_S4H_100=abc123... that SAP will accept.

 

Data Transformation for Deep Insert

One more SAP quirk: when creating an order with line items, you can't just send an items array. SAP expects a nested structure called to_Item.results. In n8n's Code node:

const args = JSON.parse(
  $input.first().json.choices[0].message.tool_calls[0].function.arguments
);
const body = { ...args };

if (body.items) {
  body.to_Item = { results: body.items };
  delete body.items;
}

return [{ json: { sapBody: body } }];

This transforms the AI's clean output into SAP's expected format.

 

A Real Example

Input Email

Hello, please create a sales order with the following details:
Customer: 17100003
Sales Org: 1710
Items: TG11 (qty 5), TG12 (qty 10)
Currency: USD

What Happens

  1. AI identifies this as create_sales_order
  2. Extracts: customer, items array, currency
  3. Workflow fetches CSRF token + session cookie
  4. Transforms items array to SAP's to_Item.results format
  5. POSTs to /sap/opu/odata/sap/API_SALES_ORDER_SRV/A_SalesOrder
  6. SAP creates order, returns details

Response Email

The user never touched SAP. They just sent an email.

What's Next

The same pattern works for anything with an OData API:

  • Invoice queries
  • Delivery status checks
  • Material lookups
  • Customer master updates

Define the function, connect the endpoint, done.

The Takeaway

AI isn't replacing SAP knowledge here - it's translating between human language and system requirements. The business logic, the validations, the actual processing - that's all still SAP. The AI just makes it accessible to people who don't speak SAP.

And honestly? That's the kind of AI use case I find most valuable. Not replacing expertise, but removing friction.

entry.title: How to call AS ABAP from SAP Datasphere
entry.content:
Introduction

For communication between SAP systems based on AS ABAP, RFC connections are the standard method. For example, when extracting from SAP ERP, the SAP BW system first launches the extractor in the source system via a BW->ERP RFC connection. The source system then returns the data collected by the extractor via an ERP->BW RFC connection.

For SAP Datasphere (hereinafter referred to as DSP), the natural successor to this approach is the BW Bridge use. However, not all projects justify using BW Bridge in terms of system landscape, architecture, project scope, and implementation costs.

This blog proposes an approach that allows DSP to execute code in a system running AS ABAP (without using BW Bridge). We will call this code as target code. The idea is to leverage CDS functionality known such as Virtual Elements. A Virtual Element is a field in a CDS View Entity, specifically defined using annotations. This field is not retrieved from the database table level and is not calculated by formulas within the CDS View itself. Instead, accessing the CDS View triggers execution of specialized ABAP code that implements the calculation logic for this field. For BW specialists this may remember well-known virtual characteristics and key figures. On the other hand, the CDS View is an object accessible to DSPs, for example, as a Source for Data Flow. Thus, by running Data Flow Run on the DSP side, we automatically initiate execution of the code that calculates the Virtual Element values ​​in AS ABAP. We can, in turn, include calls of any desired programs, functional modules, or class methods to this code—that is, achieve the execution of the target code.

Unfortunately, this approach has one significant drawback that complicates its implementation: Virtual elements are only function within OData services generated from CDS views. This means that we cannot use connections of type SAP S/4HANA On-Premise, SAP ABAP, or SAP BW on the DSP side. You will need to create a Generic OData connection.

Implementation plan is following:

  1. Create a CDS View Entity in S/4HANA using a Virtual Element.
  2. Create an ABAP class that implements the calculation of this Virtual Element. The class code will include the target code call.
  3. Register and test the OData Service.
  4. Review the permissions required for the technical user on whose behalf the connection will be made. These permissions should be broader than the standard permissions for connections of type SAP S/4HANA On-Premise or SAP ABAP.
  5. Notes regarding creating a Generic OData connection in DSP.
  6. Create a simple Data Flow in DSP using the OData connection to the created CDS View. Running this Data Flow (manually, scheduled, or as part of a Task Chain) will trigger our target code in S/4HANA.

CDS View Entity with Virtual Element

We will create a dummy CDS View. It doesn't need to provide any meaningful data. We only need the ability to add a Virtual Element field to it. Therefore, as an example, we'll use a selection from the TKA01 (Controlling Areas) table. Typically, a single Controlling Area with a value of 1000 is used. Based on this assumption, we'll create the CDS View. Of course, you can use other selections that are guaranteed to return a single record in your system. The following code is proposed:

@EndUserText.label: 'CDS Virtual Element Test'
@analytics.dataCategory: #DIMENSION
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@vdm.viewType: #BASIC
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations:true
@ObjectModel.usageType.serviceQuality: #A
@ObjectModel.usageType.sizeCategory: #S
@ObjectModel.usageType.dataClass: #MASTER
@analytics.dataExtraction.enabled: true
@analytics.dataExtraction.delta.changeDataCapture.automatic: true
@ObjectModel.supportedCapabilities:[#EXTRACTION_DATA_SOURCE]
@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@odata.publish: true

define view entity Z_CDS_VIRT_ELEM as select from tka01
{
key kokrs,
substring( cast( tstmp_current_utctimestamp() as abap.char(17) ), 1, 8 ) as dat,
@ObjectModel.readOnly: true
@ObjectModel.virtualElement: true
@ObjectModel.virtualElementCalculatedBy: 'ABAP:ZCL_CDS_VIRT_ELEM_CALC'
cast('' as abap.sstring( 1 )) as flag
}
where kokrs = '1000'
  •  The annotation at line 13 enables access to the CDS View via the OData Service.
  • The ‘dat’ field is added as a simple log: to see the date of the last calculation run.
  • The ‘flag’ field of the Virtual Element type is defined at line 22. It has the simplest form of a flag field. By default, as can be seen from the code, this field is empty. However, upon completion of calculations on the AS ABAP side, it will be filled with the value 'X'. This flag confirms at DSP side the successful completion of ABAP calculations.
  • The annotations at lines 19-21 define the "flag" field as a Virtual Element. In particular, the @ObjectModel.virtualElementCalculatedBy annotation specifies the name of the ABAP class that should calculate the ‘flag’ field.

When you initially activate this code, you'll receive, in particular, two warnings. For line 13 the system reports that the corresponding OData service is not active. For line 21 the system reports that the corresponding class doesn't exist. In the next steps, we'll resolve these issues.

If we use the context menu command 'Open With -> Data Preview' on our CDS View at this point, the result will be as follows:Here the 'kokrs' and 'dat' fields are populated using standard CDS syntax. The virtual 'flag' field is left blank.

Creation of ABAP Class implementing our calculations

A Virtual Element calculation implementing  class must use two standard interfaces: IF_SADL_EXIT и IF_SADL_EXIT_CALC_ELEMENT_READ:

class ZCL_CDS_VIRT_ELEM_CALC definition
public
final
create public .

public section.

interfaces IF_SADL_EXIT .
interfaces IF_SADL_EXIT_CALC_ELEMENT_READ .
protected section.
private section.
ENDCLASS.

Second of these interfaces has two standard methods. A detailed description of these methods and their parameters can be found in the official documentation: https://help.sap.com/docs/ABAP_PLATFORM_BW4HANA/cc0c305d2fab47bd808adcad3ca7ee9d/4b5e56ec6f28453f81a...

In our example, the following code is proposed for the GET_CALCULATION_INFO method:

METHOD if_sadl_exit_calc_element_read~get_calculation_info.
IF iv_entity = 'Z_CDS_VIRT_ELEM'.
"Here we'll list which fields of CDS View will be used in virtual element calculation
IF line_exists( it_requested_calc_elements[ table_line = 'FLAG' ] ).
APPEND 'KOKRS' TO et_requested_orig_elements.
ENDIF.
ELSE.
" Potential errors should be processed by public constants,
" described in exceptional class, inherited from CX_SADL_EXIT.
" For example, if CDS View is unknown:

RAISE EXCEPTION TYPE zcx_ds_virt_elem_exceptions EXPORTING textid = zcx_ds_virt_elem_exceptions=>c_entity_not_known.
ENDIF.
ENDMETHOD.

Errors occurring during execution of the class described above must be handled using system messages. For this purpose, public constants will be created in a separate exception class. The class must be a child of the standard CX_SADL_EXIT class. An example of such an exception class is provided in the official documentation: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_NETWEAVER_AS_ABAP_751_IP/cc0c305d2fab47bd808adcad3ca7ee9d/8cf451799476...  

As we can see, after specifying the parent class, a small constructor override is required. Each error is then declared as a public constant attribute. Naturally, such attribute contains a system message, previously defined in the Message Class relevant to the task.

Since our CDS View is designed to be as simple as possible, the IT_REQUESTED_CALC_ELEMENTS table will contain a single row with the ‘FLAG’ field. The ET_REQUESTED_ORIG_ELEMENTS table of regular source fields also contains only one record with the ‘KOKRS’ field.

Let's move on to the CALCULATE method:

METHOD if_sadl_exit_calc_element_read~calculate.
CHECK NOT it_original_data IS INITIAL.
DATA lit_calculated_data TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF z_cds_virt_elem WITH DEFAULT KEY.
MOVE-CORRESPONDING it_original_data TO lit_calculated_data.

LOOP AT lit_calculated_data ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<lfs_calculated_data>).
"Here we need to add a call to the code that we wanted to initiate from the DSP side.
"For example, we can submit some program:
" SUBMIT z_some_program AND RETURN.
<lfs_calculated_data>-flag = 'X'. "Formal flag of the calculation readiness
EXIT. "Additional guarantee that calculation will be performed only once
ENDLOOP.
MOVE-CORRESPONDING lit_calculated_data TO ct_calculated_data.
ENDMETHOD.

Our CDS View is intentionally designed to return exactly one record. This means that in the CALCULATE method, the IT_ORIGINAL_DATA table also contains exactly one record. Consequently, the LOOP/ENDLOOP loop will only be executed once, and the target code will also be called only once.

After the ZCL_CDS_VIRT_ELEM_CALC class is activated, the warning on line 21 of our CDS View code will disappear.

OData Service activation

Our CDS View annotation specifies that it can be accessed via an OData Service. When we activate the CDS code, the corresponding OData Service will be created but not activated. Activation must be performed manually in the /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE transaction. The OData Service name consists of the name of our CDS View followed by the _CDS suffix. In our example, the resulting name is Z_CDS_VIRT_ELEM_CDS.

Click the 'Add Service' button:

 An ‘Add Selected Services’ window will appear. Enter Z_CDS_VIRT_ELEM_CDS as the technical name, and select LOCAL from the list in the 'System Alias' field. Then click 'Get Services'. A line with our service should appear:

 Select the row with service description and press the ‘Add Selected Services’ button:In the 'Package Assignment' field, specify the appropriate package for subsequent import of developed objects to the production system. In the 'ICF Node' section, select the 'SAP Gateway OData V2' setting. An important setting is the 'Enable OAuth for Service' checkbox. Check with your BASIS Team to determine whether OAuth will be used when connecting the DSP to the ABAP system. If so, enable this checkbox. After confirming the settings, service activation will begin. Upon completion, the following message will appear: 

Now you need to return to the main transaction window and filter the newly activated service in the list:

 Check the traffic light in the 'Status' field (the leftmost field in the ICF Nodes section). If it's yellow, select the drop-down list from the 'ICF Node' button directly above it and execute the 'Activate' command:

The window for selecting a package for importing activated objects into production will appear again:

After this, the traffic light will change to green. You can now test the service by clicking the 'SAP Gateway Client' button:

 By default, the test transaction specifies a URI that reads the service description:

But we're interested in what entities this service can provide. This can be found by clicking the 'Entity Set' button:

Here we see that the service can read data for CDS View Z_CDS_VIRT_ELEM. By double-clicking this name, we can change the URI:

If you now click the 'Execute' button, data will be read from the CDS View Z_CDS_VIRT_ELEM via the OData Service. This is precisely the operation that should trigger the calculation of the 'flag' field via the ABAP class described above. If you need to study or debug this class, you can set breakpoints there before clicking 'Execute'. The calculation results will be presented here in XML format:Here you can see that the 'flag' field is filled with the value 'X'. This means that the CALCULATE method was executed along with the target code call.

System user authorizations

When creating a DSP connection to an ABAP system, a special user of the System type is used. Typically, the BASIS Team assigns this user a standard set of permissions sufficient for connections of SAP S/4HANA On-Premise, SAP ABAP, or SAP BW types. Experience has shown that a Generic OData connection requires a number of additional permissions.

First, you need to add authorizations to launch the created OData Service. This is the S_SERVICE authorization object. Editing it differs from most standard authorization objects. After adding the object to the role profile, click the edit button for the SRV_NAME field. In the window that appears, select 'TADIR Service' from the Type drop-down list, which will display a list of services allowed to launch:

We need to add two types of objects for our service: IWSG and IWSV. First, select the appropriate type from the list in the 'Object Type' column. Then, use the search (for example, by pressing F4) in the 'Object Name' column. In both cases, you can use the name of our service (i.e. -  Z_CDS_VIRT_ELEM_CDS) to search for the desired value:The name of the object being searched consists of the service name and the ordinal number of the active version of this service (four digits). For the first version of service in our example, the IWSG type value is 'Z_CDS_VIRT_ELEM_CDS_0001', and the IWSV type value is 'Z_CDS_VIRT_ELEM_CDS 0001'.

After saving the selected values, you will see that the value 'HT' is automatically added to the SRV_TYPE field. Thus, the S_SERVICE authorizations have been added and the values ​​specified.

It is also important to note that specific authorizations may be required, which depend on the specific target code you want to run in the CALCULATE method described above. For example, if you plan to use a command like 'SUBMIT z_some_program AND RETURN' to launch a program, you will need the corresponding authorizations in the S_PROGRAM object. If the target code performs, for example, calculations related to the FI module, the system user may require authorizations specific to that module. If the target code accesses the contents of database tables, authorizations in the S_TABU_DIS and S_TABU_NAM objects may be required. And so on. Most likely, during the OData service testing phase (as described in the previous section), you will need to collaborate with the BASIS Team to monitor missing authorizations and add them to the system user's roles. The simplest solution is to temporarily (and only in the development system!) change the user type from system to dialog. In this case, you can log in to the SAP GUI with this user's login and manually run OData service testing while simultaneously monitoring the authorizations being checked.

The security issue of assigning such non-typical authorizations to a system user is mitigated by the fact that this user is created only to connect DSP to the ABAP system and cannot be used for manual data access, like a dialog user.

DSP OData Connection

Creating a Generic OData connection should be performed by BASIS Team specialists, as the required settings depend on the system landscape used in your specific project, as well as the security settings applied (for example, whether OAuth is enabled). Here's an example of the settings used in our project:The address entered in the ‘URL’ field ends with the technical name of our OData service. Therefore, a separate connection will need to be created for each OData service.

In the ‘Version’ field, V2 is selected. This is because we specified OData V2 when activating the service above and didn't have the option to select V4:

The example also shows that OAuth is used. That's why the corresponding checkbox was enabled earlier when activating the service:

At the bottom of the connection settings window, click the ‘Show Advanced Properties’ button:

Here you need to specify for the OData service the client number of the ABAP system we are connecting to:

 

DSP Data Flow to use OData connection and trigger the target code

We need to create a Data Flow with our OData service as a source. To do this, open Data Builder (in the same Space where you created OData service connection). In the left pane, select the 'Sources' tab and open the 'Connections' folder:

If all connection settings and system user authorizations were configured correctly, you will see the connection, and when you expand it, you will see the name of our CDS View:

The icon next to the CDS View is the same as for a regular table. You can drag and drop this View into the Data Flow being created. It will be automatically recognized as a Source. At this step, you can select the 'Preview Data' command for this table:

The result should look like this:

The presence of an 'X' in the 'flag' field means that the target code has been executed on the ABAP system side. If the target code execution takes a long time, you will also notice the delay when viewing it in the DSP.

As a final step, you need to complete the Data Flow by adding the target table to it, which in this case only serves as a dummy. Save the Data Flow and deploy it.

Result

Now you can initiate execution of the target code in the ABAP system in any way convenient for you: by manually starting the Data Flow, scheduling it, or including the Data Flow in a Task Chain.

 

 

Note: Data Preview is not working for Virtual Element

As a result of our implementation, OData Service is activated and implementing ABAP class exist for our Virtual Element. And  we can repeat attempt of CDS data preview in ABAP Development Tools (CDS editor context menu command 'Open With -> Data Preview'). Unfortunately, result will be the same: field ‘flag’ is empty: 

This is explained by the fact that the Virtual Element calculation is performed only when accessing the CDS through OData Service.

entry.title: SAP BTP - APIM - Domain-Centric Routing Pattern (DCRP): Governing APIs via CPI Provider - Part I
entry.content:
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

The Problem You're Facing:
Your API Management environment is drowning in chaos. Every new integration creates a separate API Proxy. 100 integrations = 100 proxies. Each takes 15 minutes to deploy, has its own security policies, monitoring dashboard, and versioning strategy. Your team spends more time managing infrastructure than delivering business value.

This is "Proxy Sprawl" — and it's killing your enviornaments ?

The Business Impact:

  • Deployment bottlenecks: 15 minutes per proxy × 100 integrations = 25 hours of repetitive work
  • Ungoverned chaos: No naming standards, scattered monitoring, security policy inconsistencies
  • Zero scalability: Adding one new integration requires deploying an entirely new proxy

The Solution: DCRP (Domain-Centric Routing Pattern)
Instead of 100 separate proxies, you deploy one proxy per business domain. A metadata-driven routing engine (JavaScript + KeyValueMap) dynamically routes requests to the correct backend service—without hardcoding endpoints or redeploying infrastructure.

Think of it as: One smart gateway per business domain (Sales, Finance, Logistics) instead of hundreds of dumb pipes.

The Proof (Real Deployment Results):

  • 4 business domains managing 8 backend services deployed in 8 minutes total
  • 96% faster than traditional 1:1 proxy model (8 min vs 120 min)
  • Add new integration in 30 seconds (update metadata only—no redeployment)
  • One JavaScript routing engine reused across all domains (zero code duplication)

My Contribution:
I didn't invent dynamic routing—SAP APIM, AWS, Azure, and Kong all have it. I created the governance methodology that makes it work at enterprise scale: naming conventions, KVM structure, error handling patterns, analytics frameworks, and deployment templates.

Ready to escape proxy hell? Keep reading.

 

Blog Enhancement: This article has been significantly revised with

Last Updated: February 1, 2026 - Ricardo Viana - "The Commander"

 This revision includes:

  • 25 professional diagrams.
  •  Step-by-step visual flows.
  • 60% more concise explanations.
  • Complete JavaScript code provided in expandable sections.

  • EXECUTIVE SUMMARY
  • Last Updated: February 1, 2026 - Ricardo Viana - "The Commander"
  • The DCRP (Domain-Centric Routing Pattern) : Solving Proxy Sprawl at Enterprise Scale
  • Introduction
  • 1 - The "Sprawl" Problem - An Overview
  • 2 - What is DCRP (Domain Centric Routing Pattern) ?
  • 2.3 - Integration Diagram Overview:
  • 2.4 - DCRP in Context: Pattern vs Technology
  • 2.4.1 - The Technical Foundation: KVM + JavaScript
  • 2.5 -My Contribution: From Principles to Methodology
  • 2.6 - DCRP Across API Management Platforms
  • 2.7 - Proxy Sprawl vs DCRP
  • 3 - The API Proxy Design Pattern provides:
  • 4 - Implementation: Configuring the DCRP API Proxy
  • 5 - The Semantic Facade: One Proxy, Multiple Intentions
  • 6 - The Semantic Bridge: Public API to Technical Endpoints
  • 7 - The Routing Engine: Understanding the API-Proxy Execution Flow
  • 7.2 - Why This Sequence Matters for DCRP
  • 7.3 - The DCRP Brain executes 4 policy stages:
  • 8 - The Brain Components: Key Value Mapping and JavaScript Logic
  • 8.1 - Key-Value Map (KVM): The Memory
  • 8.2 - Scaling the Pattern: Two Levels of High-Scale Flexibility
  • Deployment times assume:
  • 8.6 - The Template Philosophy: Scaling Strategies
  • 8.7 - When Domain Templates Win:
  • Break-even point:5 instances per domain
  • 9 - The Domain Template Contract:
  • 9.1 - Deployment Checklist (Domain Template Strategy):
  • 9.2 JavaScript Policy – Dissecting the DCRP Routing Engine
  • 9.3- The Complete JavaScript Code
  • 9.4 - How the Routing Engine Works
  • 10 -API Contracts, Versioning & Openapi Management
  • 10.1 - Why DCRP Proxies Don't Need Versioning
  • 10.2 - The Exception: Breaking Changes in Proxy Policies
  • 10.3 - OpenAPI Management: The Hybrid Approach
  • 10.4 - KVM vs OpenAPI: Complementary, Not Redundant
  • 11 -Statistics Collector – Capturing Business Intelligence
  • Conclusion

The DCRP (Domain-Centric Routing Pattern) : Solving Proxy Sprawl at Enterprise Scale

The DCRP (Domain-Centric Routing Pattern) is a semantic name that I created to describe a governance methodology designed to solve "Proxy Sprawl" and "CPI Package Sprawl" in large-scale SAP Integration landscapes. Rather than being a new technical innovation, it is an architectural approach leveraging native SAP APIM dynamic routing capabilities to create a business-centric domain layer.

My goal is to bridge high-level governance with hands-on execution, to ensure clarity for all technical levels, I have manually designed visual integration diagrams and step-by-step guides to make this pattern accessible to everyone.
